**READ ME**

**WOMEN PROTECTION SERVICES**

**Roll number: BSCS24124**  
                       **BSCS24128**

**Name:	           Zayna Farhan**  
           **Laiba Yasir**

**Project overview**  
In case of distressful situation for women such as domestic violence, sexual assault, harassment, stalking, kidnapping, or a medical crisis they often dont know where to turn to.This member based system helps to resolve such a situation. It allows victims to look at the real time progress being taken against their case and also provides future support such as law cases and mental health.  
The system sits at the in between the world of public safety, social welfare, and emergency response  
A victim registered on the system can log a request for help at any time. That request is picked up by a dispatcher who reviews the situation, verifies the nature of the emergency, and creates an incident record. Depending on the severity, the dispatcher can coordinate a response that might involve police services, an ambulance,and volunteers who are assigned based on their availability.  
Every incident is tracked with a status: pending, ongoing, or completed so nothing falls through the cracks. Once the immediate emergency is handled, the system supports the victim further by connecting her to legal representation through a law case record, and arranging follow up support through referred centers for counseling

**Tech stack**

| Layer | Technology |
| :---- | :---- |
| Frontend Framework | React.js (Create React App) |
| Frontend Styling | CSS |
| Backend Framework | Node.js \+ Express.js |
| Database | MySQL |
| DB Access | mysql2 (connection pool) |
| Authentication | JWT (jsonwebtoken) |
| Password Security | bcrypt |
| Environment Config | dotenv |
| Cross-Origin Handling | cors |
| Request Parsing | body-parser |

**System Architecture**

### **The Frontend:** The React app runs in the user's browser and is responsible entirely for what the user sees and interacts with. When a user performs an action such as  logging in, submitting an emergency request, checking an incident status. The React app does not handle that logic itself. Instead, it packages the relevant data and sends an HTTP request to the backend through the API utility functions stored in the `api` folder. It then waits for a response and updates the UI accordingly. The frontend has no direct knowledge of the database.

### **The Backend:** The Express server is the main working area of the application. It listens for incoming HTTP requests from the frontend on port 5000 and routes each request to the correct handler based on the URL and the user's role. Before any protected route is executed, the request passes through the `verifyJWT` middleware which checks the token, and then through `allowRoles` which confirms the user has permission to perform that action. Once authorized, the route handler runs its business logic, queries the MySQL database through the connection pool, and sends a structured JSON response back to the frontend. The backend also handles password hashing, token generation, and error responses for unknown routes.

### **The Database:** The MySQL database is the persistence layer. It stores all application data across its normalized tables such as users, victims, dispatchers, volunteers, incidents, assignments, police services, ambulances, law cases, and follow-up support records. The backend communicates with it through a connection pool, which keeps multiple connections open and reuses them efficiently rather than opening a new connection on every request. The database also enforces its own logic independently through triggers, for example, automatically setting a completion timestamp when an assignment is marked done, or blocking a volunteer assignment if the volunteer is unavailable.

**UI examples**

**![][image1]**

Login page  because every user role (victim, dispatcher, volunteer, admin) enters the system here, and it's where JWT authentication begins.  
It works by taking user id and password and checking against the data in the database to see if it is valid. In case of registration it takes victims information such as CNIC, phone number address etc. in case that a user forgets their id then they can post an emergency request based on their email and id and later ask for password change.

![][image2]  
Victim dashboard: it shows the information and progress of the victims case to the victim. It also allows the victim to place emergency requests by pressing submit new emergency requests and filling in the form. The page reloads every 30 seconds so the victim doesn't have to keep on reloading. It also provides different filters to search for particular requests.

![][image3]

Dispatchers dashboard: it shows to the dispatcher his total number of incidents, calls, cases, resources, volunteers assigned and backup. It also provides average response time and filters to search for certain cases etc. it allows dispatchers to change their availability.  
Dispatchers can create new incidents, call law cases etc by pressing the associated create button and filling in the required information in the form. It also allows dispatchers to assign police volunteers etc.

**Setup & Installation Guide**

# **Prerequisites**

The following must be installed and running on your machine before setup begins. Node.js v18 or above is required to run both the backend server and the React frontend. npm comes bundled with Node.js and handles all package installation. MySQL must be installed and actively running as the database engine. Once the backend is configured, it must be running on port 5000 before the frontend can communicate with it.

**Frontend Setup**

Navigate to the project root folder and run the following commands in order:

npx create-react-app frontend  
cd frontend  
npm install axios react-router-dom jspdf xlsx jspdf-autotable  
npm install react-icons

Create a `.env` file inside the `frontend/` folder with the following content:

REACT\_APP\_API\_URL=http://localhost:5000/api

The `src/` folder should be organised with the following subfolders:

* `api/` — Axios instance and API configuration  
* `components/` — shared reusable components  
* `context/` — authentication context and state  
* `pages/` — all role-based dashboard pages

**Backend Setup**

Navigate to the project root folder and install all backend dependencies:

npm install

Create a `.env` file in the root backend folder with the following content:

DB\_HOST=localhost  
DB\_USER=root  
DB\_PASSWORD=yourpassword  
DB\_NAME=womenprotectionservicesdb  
DB\_CONNECTION\_LIMIT=10  
PORT=5000

`DB_HOST` is the address of your MySQL server, which for local development is localhost. `DB_USER` and `DB_PASSWORD` are your MySQL credentials. `DB_NAME` must match exactly what the schema file creates. `DB_CONNECTION_LIMIT` controls how many simultaneous connections the pool maintains. `PORT` is the port the Express server listens on and must match the URL configured in the frontend `.env`.

Start the backend server with:

node server.js

**Database Setup**

Open your MySQL client and run the two SQL files in the following order. The schema must be run first as the seed data depends on the tables already existing:

**Start the Frontend**

In a separate terminal, navigate to the `frontend/` folder and start the React development server:

cd frontend  
npm start

The application will open automatically in your browser at `http://localhost:3000`.

**Running the Full Application**

Two terminals must remain open simultaneously for the application to function. MySQL must already be running before either server is started.

| Terminal | Location | Command | Address |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Terminal 1 | project root | node server.js | localhost:5000 |
| Terminal 2 | frontend/ | npm start | localhost:3000 |

## **System Roles**

The system defines four roles: Victim, Dispatcher, Volunteer, and Admin. A user can hold multiple roles simultaneously for example a volunteer can also be a victim.

**1\. Victim**

How a user becomes one: Registered via `POST /api/auth/register` (auto-assigned), or promoted by an Admin.

Can do:

* Submit emergency requests (`POST /api/victim/request`)  
* View their own incidents and case history (`GET /api/victim/incidents`) via the victim\_personal\_view view  
* Register as a victim if not already one (`POST /api/victim/register`)  
* Submit a public emergency without logging in (`POST /api/victim/emergency`) using only user\_id \+ email  
* Request password recovery (`POST /api/password-recovery`)  
* View their own recovery requests (`GET /api/password-recovery/my-requests`)

Cannot do:

* Access any dispatcher, admin, or volunteer routes  
* View other victims' data  
* Manage incidents, assignments, volunteers, or resources

Logout behavior: Never blocked — always allowed to log out immediately.

**2\. Dispatcher**

How a user becomes one: Created by an Admin via `POST /api/admin/user` with `role: "Dispatcher"`, requiring a `centre_id`.

Can do:

* Manage their own workload and availability (`GET /api/dispatcher/workload`, `PUT /api/dispatcher/availability`)  
* Search for existing users to find registered victims (`GET /api/dispatcher/victim/search`) — if a found user is not yet registered as a victim, the dispatcher is shown a message directing them to ask an Admin to register that person first before a call can be logged against them  
* Update victim info (`PUT /api/dispatcher/victim/:id`)  
* Log incoming calls (`POST /api/dispatcher/call-log`, view via `GET /api/dispatcher/calls`) — calls can only be logged against users already registered as victims in the system  
* Create and update incidents (`POST /api/dispatcher/incident`, `PUT /api/dispatcher/incident/:id`)  
* Create resource assignments (police \+ ambulance) (`POST /api/dispatcher/assignment`)  
* Assign and remove volunteers from any centre (`POST /api/dispatcher/incident/:id/assign-volunteers`, `DELETE /api/dispatcher/volunteer-assignment/:id`) — available volunteers across all centres are shown as options; volunteers can only be removed while the assignment is still Pending, which resets their availability to Yes  
* Process volunteer backup requests (`POST /api/dispatcher/volunteer/:id/backup-request`)  
* Create and update law cases (`POST /api/dispatcher/lawcase`, `PUT /api/dispatcher/lawcase/:id`)  
* Create and update follow-up support (`POST /api/dispatcher/followup`, `PUT /api/dispatcher/followup/:id`)  
* View available resources, volunteers across all centres, centres, and backup status

Cannot do:

* Access admin routes (user management, table management, system locks)  
* Access incidents or calls not assigned to them  
* Modify other dispatchers' records

### **3\. Volunteer**

How a user becomes one: Created by an Admin via `POST /api/admin/user` with `role: "Volunteer"`, requiring a `centre_id` and `emergency_contact`.

Can do:

* View their current active assignments (`GET /api/volunteer/assignments/current`)  
* View their completed assignment history (`GET /api/volunteer/assignments/history`)  
* Update their own status on an assignment (`PUT /api/volunteer/assignment/:id`) — values: `Arrived`, `Ongoing`, `Pending`  
* Update their own availability (`PUT /api/volunteer/availability`) blocked if they have active assignments  
* Request backup on an active assignment (`POST /api/volunteer/assignment/:id/request-backup`), which appends a note to the incident visible to the dispatcher  
* Submit a public emergency without logging in (`POST /api/victim/emergency/volunteer`)  auto-registers them as a Victim reusing their existing emergency contact

Cannot do:

* Access dispatcher or admin routes  
* Directly modify incidents, assignments, or resources  
* View other volunteers' assignments

Logout behavior: Blocked if they have any non-Completed assignments. Availability is set to `No` and Status cleared on successful logout.

### **4\. Admin**

How a user becomes one: Created by another Admin via `POST /api/admin/user` with `role: "Admin"`. Must be 18+.

Can do:

User Management:

* Create users of any role (`POST /api/admin/user`)  
* Update any user's info or change their role (`PUT /api/admin/user/:id`)  
* Delete users with safety checks (no open incidents, law cases, follow-ups, or pending recovery requests) (`DELETE /api/admin/user/:id`)  
* Search and list all users (`GET /api/admin/users`, `GET /api/admin/users/search`)

Table Management:

* View all 15 tables and individual records (`GET /api/admin/:tablename`, `GET /api/admin/:tablename/:id`)  
* Update and delete records in: `Request_log`, `Law_case`, `Follow_up_support`, `Centre`, `Incident`, `Assignment`, `Ambulance_service`, `Police_services`

System / Concurrency:

* Claim and release lock that blocks dispatcher writes  
* Check lock status (`GET /api/admin/system/lock-status`)  
* Take over any incident lock from a dispatcher (`POST /api/dispatcher/incident/:id/admin-takeover`)  
* Override any row-level lock held by a dispatcher

Oversight:

* View all dispatchers, volunteers, victims, and incidents across all centres (`GET /api/admin/dispatchers`, `/volunteers`, `/victims`, `/incidents`)  
* Set a dispatcher's availability (`PUT /api/admin/dispatcher/:id/availability`)  
* Reassign an incident to a different dispatcher (`PUT /api/admin/incident/:id/reassign`)

Password Recovery:

* View all recovery requests (`GET /api/admin/recoveries`)  
* View their own assigned requests (`GET /api/admin/recoveries/mine`)  
* Reset a user's password for requests assigned to them specifically (`PUT /api/admin/recovery/:id/reset-password`)  
* Delete recovery requests (`DELETE /api/admin/recovery/:id`)

Cannot do:

* The last remaining Admin cannot be deleted  
* An Admin referenced in Password\_Recovery records cannot be deleted until those records are cleared  
* An Admin with a role in Password\_Recovery as `Admin_id` cannot be deleted while those records exist

Logout behavior: Blocked if they have any `Pending` Password\_Recovery requests assigned to them. Availability is set to `No` on successful logout (this is required for the round-robin recovery assignment system to exclude offline admins).

### **Public Endpoints (No Login Required)**

These work for any user who has forgotten their password but needs help urgently:

**`POST /api/victim/verify-identity`**  Anyone. Confirms user\_id \+ email \+ role combination before proceeding to an emergency flow.

**`POST /api/victim/emergency`**  Victims. Submits an emergency request using user\_id \+ email only, no JWT needed.

**`POST /api/victim/emergency/volunteer`**  Volunteers. Same flow but auto-reuses emergency contact from the Volunteer table.

**`POST /api/victim/emergency/admin`** Admins. Requires emergency\_contact only if not already registered as a Victim.

**`POST /api/victim/emergency/dispatcher`** Dispatchers. Same as admin flow; assigns request to a different available dispatcher.

**`POST /api/password-recovery/forgot`**  Anyone. Submits a password recovery request using user\_id \+ email, assigned to an available admin via round-robin.

# **Feature Walkthrough**

## **Authentication & Registration**

**Self-Registration (Victim only)** Any new user can register as a victim at `/register`. The form collects name, email, phone, CNIC, date of birth, address, emergency contact, and password. On success the backend returns a JWT and a User ID which the user must save as it's required for every login. Maps to `POST /auth/register` and `Register.jsx`.

**Login (All roles)** All roles (Admin, Dispatcher, Volunteer, Victim) log in from the same `/login` page using User ID and password. The backend detects which role tables the user belongs to and encodes all roles into the JWT. Dispatchers who are also Victims get both roles in the token. Maps to `POST /auth/login` and `Login.jsx`.

**Logout** Logout is not instant as  it checks whether the role has unresolved obligations first. Admins are blocked if they have pending password recovery requests assigned to them. Dispatchers are blocked if they have unprocessed emergency requests, active incidents, or active assignments. Volunteers are blocked if they have any non-completed assignments. Victims are never blocked. On success the backend sets `Availability = No` for the relevant role. Maps to `POST /auth/logout` in `authRoutes.js`.

**Forgot Password (Public)** From the login screen any user can submit a recovery request using their User ID and email with no JWT needed. The backend round-robins the request to an available admin. If no admin is available the request is rejected. Maps to `POST /password-recovery/forgot` and the Forgot screen in `Login.jsx`.

**Emergency Request Without Login (Public)** For users who are in danger but cannot log in. The login screen has a prominent "Request Emergency Help" button that opens a two-step flow. Step 1 verifies identity via `POST /victim/verify-identity` using User ID, email, and role selection. The other step collects note and location, with emergency contact asked only for Admin and Dispatcher roles if they are not already registered as a Victim. Volunteers never need to provide one as their existing emergency contact is reused. Four separate endpoints handle the four roles: `POST /victim/emergency`, `/emergency/volunteer`, `/emergency/admin`, `/emergency/dispatcher`. All implemented in `Login.jsx`.

## **Victim Features**

**Submit Emergency Request (Authenticated)** From the Victim dashboard's Submit tab, a logged-in victim provides a note and location. The backend round-robins the request to an available dispatcher excluding the victim's own ID. Maps to `POST /victim/request` and `VictimDashboard.jsx`.

**Track Incidents** The Incidents tab shows the victim's full case history pulled from the `victim_personal_view` view. Each card shows a tage progress pipeline (Request Submitted → Incident Verified → Response Dispatched → Case Closed) so the victim can see exactly where their case stands. The view auto-refreshes every 30 seconds. Pressing on a card reveals full details including dispatcher name, incident assessment, assignment status, police and ambulance info, legal case, and follow-up support. Maps to `GET /victim/incidents`.

**Register as Victim (Cross-role)** Dispatchers and Volunteers and Admin who are not already victims can register as one from their own dashboards. Volunteers reuse their existing emergency contact automatically. Dispatchers are asked to provide one. On success the JWT is refreshed with the Victim role added, and a Victim tab appears in the dashboard. Maps to `POST /victim/register`.

**Dispatcher Features**

**Workload Overview** The Overview tab shows the dispatcher's current active request and incident counts, oldest unresolved request timestamp, historical totals, and average response time. The dispatcher can toggle their own availability here. Maps to `GET /dispatcher/workload` and `PUT /dispatcher/availability`.

**Call Logging** After identifying the victim the dispatcher logs the call with a note, location, and type (Emergency, Query, or False). This creates a Request\_log record. The dispatcher can only log calls against victims who are already registered in the system. If the person is found but not yet registered as a victim, the dispatcher is shown a message to contact an admin to register them first. Maps to `POST /dispatcher/call-log`. Calls can be viewed, filtered, and edited from the Calls tab.

**Incident Management** From unprocessed calls the dispatcher creates incidents by selecting emergency type, severity, verification status, and optionally overriding location and note. Incidents can be updated (status, severity, verification, note) and viewed with full assignment and resource details. Maps to `POST /dispatcher/incident`, `PUT /dispatcher/incident/:id`, `GET /dispatcher/incidents`.

**Resource Assignment** The dispatcher creates an assignment for an incident by optionally selecting a police centre and ambulance from the available pool. Assignment status can be updated to Pending, Ongoing, or Completed — completing it requires an inline confirmation step before the change is applied. Maps to `POST /dispatcher/assignment` and `PUT /dispatcher/assignment/:id`.

**Volunteer Assignment and Remova**l Available volunteers across all centres are shown as checkboxes. Multiple can be selected and assigned to an incident's assignment. Once assigned, the volunteer's availability is set to No and status to Ongoing. Volunteers can be removed while the assignment is still Pending — doing so resets their availability to Yes. Maps to `POST /dispatcher/incident/:id/assign-volunteers` and `DELETE /dispatcher/volunteer-assignment/:id`.

**Backup Request Handling** The Backups tab shows all ongoing incidents where a volunteer has appended a backup request to the incident note. The dispatcher can see the volunteer's name, timestamp, and reason for each request, along with current police and ambulance status. Maps to `GET /dispatcher/backups`.

**Law Case Creation and Management From** any incident the dispatcher can open a law case, selecting a case type (Support or Penalty) and a law firm. Previously used firm names appear in a dropdown for convenience, with an option to enter a custom name. Law cases can have their firm name and case type updated. Maps to `POST /dispatcher/lawcase`, `PUT /dispatcher/lawcase/:id`, `GET /dispatcher/lawcases`.

**Follow-up Support** After an assignment is created the dispatcher can create a follow-up support record referring the victim to a welfare centre. Previously used centre names appear in a dropdown, with an option to enter a custom name. Leaving the field blank auto-assigns the most recently used centre. Follow-ups can have their referred centre and case type updated. Maps to `POST /dispatcher/followup`, `PUT /dispatcher/followup/:id`.

**Resource and Volunteer Overview** Dedicated tabs show all police centres and ambulances, and all volunteers across all centres with their current availability and assignment counts. Resources are filterable by location. Volunteers are filterable by availability and centre.

## **Volunteer Features**

**View Active Assignments** The Active tab shows all non-completed assignments from the `volunteer_assigned_view`, including incident details, victim name and phone, dispatcher name, police and ambulance status, and follow-up centre. Maps to `GET /volunteer/assignments/current`.

**Update Own Status** Within each active assignment card the volunteer can set their status to Pending, Arrived, or Ongoing. The UI updates optimistically immediately, then confirms once the server responds. Maps to `PUT /volunteer/assignment/:id`.

**Toggle Availability** The availability toggle in the header lets the volunteer mark themselves as available or unavailable. Toggling to unavailable is blocked if any active assignments exist. Maps to `PUT /volunteer/availability`.

**Request Backup** Each active assignment card has a "Request Backup" button. The volunteer types an optional reason and submits. This appends a timestamped backup note to the incident record, which appears in the dispatcher's Backups tab. Maps to `POST /volunteer/assignment/:id/request-backup`.

**Assignment History** The Completed tab shows all finished assignments for the volunteer's reference. Maps to `GET /volunteer/assignments/history`.

## **Admin Features**

**System Overview** The Overview tab shows counts for all entity types (victims, volunteers, dispatchers, admins, requests, incidents, assignments, law cases, follow-ups, pending recoveries), breakdowns of dispatcher and volunteer availability, and incidents by severity. Maps to `GET /admin/overview`.

**Password Recovery Management** The Recoveries tab lists all password recovery requests with user name, email, phone, request time, and assigned admin. Each admin can only reset passwords for requests assigned to them. Resetting generates a random 12-character hex password, updates the user's hash, marks the request Processed, and returns the new password to display on screen. Any admin can delete any request. Maps to `GET /admin/recoveries`, `PUT /admin/recovery/:id/reset-password`, `DELETE /admin/recovery/:id`.

**Dispatcher Oversight** The Dispatchers tab shows each dispatcher's centre, availability, total requests, total incidents, and active incident count. Admins can toggle dispatcher availability. Setting a dispatcher offline is blocked if they have active incidents or assignments — the UI then offers a reassign-all dropdown to move their open incidents to another dispatcher. Maps to `GET /admin/dispatchers`, `PUT /admin/dispatcher/:id/availability`, `PUT /admin/incident/:id/reassign`.

**Volunteer Oversight** The Volunteers tab shows all volunteers across all centres with availability, status, and assignment counts. Filterable by availability, centre, and name. Read-only for the admin. Maps to `GET /admin/volunteers`.

**Victim Oversight** The Victims tab shows all victims with their contact details, active incident count, and total requests. Filterable by active incident status and name search. Maps to `GET /admin/victims`.

**Incident Oversight** The Incidents tab shows all incidents across all dispatchers with severity, status, victim, dispatcher, and centre. Active incidents can be reassigned to a different dispatcher via dropdown. Maps to `GET /admin/incidents`, `PUT /admin/incident/:id/reassign`.

**Priority Lock** The System tab lets an admin activate write lock that blocks dispatcher write operations for a configurable duration (1–60 minutes). While active the admin retains full write access. The lock can be released early. A separate in-memory application-level lock also prevents concurrent admin writes. Maps to `POST /admin/priority-lock`, `POST /admin/priority-unlock`, `GET /admin/priority-lock-status`.

**User Management (Full CRUD)** Admins can create users of any role (Admin, Dispatcher, Volunteer, Victim) with full validation including age requirements (18+ for non-victims), CNIC format, phone uniqueness, and centre assignment. Existing users can be updated including role changes. Users can be deleted subject to guards: no open incidents, no active assignments, no pending law cases or follow-ups, no pending password recovery requests. Deleting the last admin is blocked. Maps to `POST /admin/user`, `PUT /admin/user/:id`, `DELETE /admin/user/:id`, `GET /admin/users`, `GET /admin/users/search`.

**Table Browser (under Admin Features)**

Fourteen tables support inline field editing and row deletion directly from this view  all tables except Admin, which is excluded to prevent privilege escalation. Each editable row shows both an Edit and a Del button. Deletion is gated behind the same priority lock as editing and triggers a confirmation dialog before proceeding.

User Management is fully exposed in a dedicated Users tab. Admins can search and filter all users by name, email, phone, or role, create new users of any role via a modal form, edit any user's details inline, and delete users subject to the existing safety guards. Role changes require deleting and recreating the user.

**Transaction Scenarios**

1\. User Registration (POST /auth/register — authRoutes.js)

This transaction is triggered when a new user submits the public registration form. The operation bundles two inserts atomically: one into the User table and one into the Victim table. The rationale is that a User row without a corresponding Victim row would leave the account in a broken, roleless state. A rollback occurs if the email is already registered, if the CNIC is already registered, or if any database error arises during either insert. The transaction uses a dedicated connection obtained from the pool, and the lock is released in the finally block regardless of outcome.

2\. Admin Creates a User (POST /admin/user — admin.js)

This transaction is triggered when an admin submits the user creation form from the admin panel. Before the transaction opens, the route acquires a named MySQL advisory lock using GET\_LOCK on the key "admin\_write\_lock" to prevent concurrent admin writes from interfering. Once the lock is held, a transaction bundles the insert into the User table together with a role-specific insert into whichever of Victim, Volunteer, Dispatcher, or Admin applies. For a Volunteer, the Centre table is also checked within the same connection before the transaction starts. A rollback occurs if required fields are missing, if the role is invalid, if the email, phone number, or CNIC already belongs to another user, if a Volunteer or Dispatcher references a Centre that does not exist, or if the age requirement is not met for roles that require the user to be at least 18\. The named lock is released in the finally block via RELEASE\_LOCK regardless of whether the transaction committed or rolled back.

3\. Admin Updates a User (PUT /admin/user/:id — admin.js)

This transaction is triggered when an admin edits an existing user record. Before the transaction opens, the lockRow middleware acquires a row-level advisory lock on the specific User record using GET\_LOCK with the key "User\_{id}". This prevents another dispatcher or admin from editing the same record simultaneously, and because admin always has priority, if another connection holds the lock the admin's acquireRowLock call will force-release it first. Once the lock is held, the transaction bundles all User table field updates together with any role table changes. If the role is being changed, the old role row is deleted and a new one is inserted for the new role in the same transaction. If the role stays the same, only the relevant role-specific fields such as emergency contact or centre are updated. A rollback occurs if the phone number, email, or CNIC already belongs to another user, if a role change references a nonexistent Centre, if age validation fails, or on any database error. The row-level lock is released and the connection is returned to the pool in the finally block.

4\. Admin Deletes a User (DELETE /admin/user/:id — admin.js)

This transaction is triggered when an admin removes a user account. The lockRow middleware acquires a row-level advisory lock on the User record before the transaction opens, using the same admin priority override logic as the update. The transaction bundles the deletion of the Volunteer\_assignment rows for completed assignments, followed by deletes from the Victim, Volunteer, Dispatcher, and Admin tables, and finally the User row itself. A rollback occurs if the victim has open incidents, if the dispatcher has open incidents, if the volunteer has active assignments, if there are ongoing law cases or follow-up support records tied to the user, if there is a pending password recovery request for that user, or if the user is the last remaining admin. All of these checks are performed before the transaction opens so that the rollback is reserved only for genuine database errors.

5\. Admin Resets a Password via Recovery (PUT /admin/recovery/:id/reset-password — admin.js)

This transaction is triggered when an admin processes an assigned password recovery request from the admin dashboard. The transaction bundles three operations: selecting the Password\_Recovery row with FOR UPDATE to lock it, updating the User password with a newly generated random 12-character hex string after hashing, and marking the Password\_Recovery record as Processed with a Response\_time timestamp. A rollback occurs if the recovery record does not exist, if it has already been processed, if the requesting admin is not the admin assigned to that specific request, or if no user is found with the email on the recovery record.

6\. Public Forgot Password Request (POST /password-recovery/forgot — passwordRecovery.js)

This transaction is triggered when a logged-out user submits the public forgot password form using their user ID and email. The transaction selects the last assigned Admin\_id from Password\_Recovery using FOR UPDATE to establish a consistent round-robin starting point, then locks the chosen admin row in the Admin table with FOR UPDATE to guard against a race condition where the admin might log out between being selected and the insert completing. It then inserts a new Password\_Recovery record with a status of Pending and the assigned admin's ID. A rollback occurs if the user ID and email combination does not match, if there is already a pending recovery request for that email, if no admins are currently available, or if the chosen admin became unavailable between selection and the lock being acquired.

7\. Authenticated Password Recovery Request (POST /password-recovery — passwordRecovery.js)

This transaction follows the same structure as the public forgot password flow, but is triggered by an already-logged-in Victim, Volunteer, or Dispatcher. The same round-robin admin selection logic applies, the same FOR UPDATE lock on the chosen admin row is used, and the same rollback conditions apply. The key difference is that identity is confirmed from the JWT rather than from a user ID and email pair in the request body.

8\. Admin Updates a Recovery Request (PUT /password-recovery/:id — passwordRecovery.js)

This transaction is triggered when an admin updates the status or note on a password recovery record without resetting the password. The transaction selects the record with FOR UPDATE, validates that it has not already been processed, and then applies the status and note updates along with a Response\_time timestamp. A rollback occurs if the record is not found, if it has already been processed, or on any database error.

9\. Admin Deletes a Recovery Request (DELETE /password-recovery/:id — passwordRecovery.js)

This transaction is triggered when an admin removes a recovery request entirely. The transaction selects the record with FOR UPDATE to prevent concurrent deletion, then issues the delete. A rollback occurs if the record is not found.

10\. Dispatcher Logs a Call (POST /dispatcher/call-log — dispatcher.js)

This transaction is triggered when a dispatcher creates a new call record after identifying the victim. The transaction contains a single insert into Request\_log, bundled with validation checks that run beforehand on the same connection. A rollback occurs if the victim does not exist in the Victim table, if the dispatcher's availability is set to No, or on any database error. The workload check is informational and does not block the transaction.

11\. Dispatcher Creates an Incident (POST /dispatcher/incident — dispatcher.js)

This transaction is triggered when a dispatcher escalates a logged call into a formal incident. Before the transaction opens, the route acquires a row-level advisory lock on the Request\_log record to prevent another operation from touching the same request simultaneously. Inside the transaction, a single insert is made into the Incident table. A rollback occurs if the request is not found or does not belong to the requesting dispatcher, if an incident already exists for that request, if the emergency type or severity values are invalid, or on any database error. The Request\_log lock is released in the finally block.

12\. Dispatcher Creates an Assignment (POST /dispatcher/assignment — dispatcher.js) This transaction is triggered when a dispatcher assigns optional police and ambulance resources, and optionally volunteers, to an existing incident. The route acquires a row-level advisory lock on the Incident record before the transaction opens. Inside the transaction, the Assignment row is created with whichever police and ambulance values were provided (both are optional and can be null), and volunteers are assigned one by one. Each volunteer is locked individually using GET\_LOCK("Volunteer\_{id}") within the transaction and their Availability is set to No and Status to Ongoing after a FOR UPDATE re-read confirms they are still available. A rollback occurs if the incident does not belong to the dispatcher, if an assignment already exists for that incident, if a volunteer became unavailable between the availability check and the lock, or on any database error. All volunteer locks are released inside the finally blocks of their individual lock-acquire-try blocks, and the Incident lock is released in the outer finally.

13\. Dispatcher Assigns Additional Volunteers (POST /dispatcher/incident/:id/assign-volunteers — dispatcher.js)

This transaction is triggered when a dispatcher adds volunteers to an incident that already has an assignment, or creates a minimal assignment if none exists yet. The lockRow middleware acquires a row-level advisory lock on the Incident record. Inside the transaction, if no Assignment row exists one is created, and then the assignVolunteers helper runs the same per-volunteer locking logic described above. The same rollback conditions apply as in the full assignment creation flow.

14\. Dispatcher Creates a Law Case (POST /dispatcher/lawcase — dispatcher.js) 

This transaction is triggered when a dispatcher opens a legal case against an incident. The route acquires a row-level advisory lock on the Incident record before the transaction opens. Inside the transaction, a single insert is made into the Law\_case table. A rollback occurs if the incident does not belong to the dispatcher, if a law case already exists for that incident, or on any database error. The Incident lock is released in the finally block.

15\. Dispatcher Creates Follow-Up Support (POST /dispatcher/followup — dispatcher.js)

This transaction is triggered when a dispatcher opens a follow-up support record for a completed or ongoing assignment. The route acquires a row-level advisory lock on the Assignment record before the transaction opens. If no referred centre is provided, the route queries the Follow\_up\_support table for the most recently used one and auto-assigns it before the lock is taken. Inside the transaction, a single insert is made into the Follow\_up\_support table with a status of Pending. A rollback occurs if the assignment does not belong to the dispatcher, if a follow-up record already exists for that assignment, or on any database error. The Assignment lock is released in the finally block.

16\. Dispatcher Processes a Backup Request (POST /dispatcher/volunteer/:id/backup-request — dispatcher.js)

This transaction is triggered when a dispatcher acts on a backup request raised by a volunteer in the field. Inside the transaction, the route checks for available police units and ambulances and updates their statuses to Ongoing if they are assigned, updates the Assignment row to reference the newly assigned resource IDs, and appends a timestamped backup note to the Incident's Note field using CONCAT. A rollback occurs if the volunteer is not found on an active Ongoing assignment belonging to the requesting dispatcher, or on any database error.

17\. Dispatcher Removes a Volunteer from an Assignment (DELETE /dispatcher/volunteer-assignment/:id — dispatcher.js)

This transaction is triggered when a dispatcher removes a volunteer from a Pending assignment before it goes Ongoing. Inside the transaction, the Volunteer\_assignment row is deleted and the volunteer's Availability is reset to Yes and Status is cleared. A rollback occurs if the record is not found, if it does not belong to the requesting dispatcher, if the assignment status is already Ongoing or Completed, or on any database error.

18\. Victim Registers Themselves as a Victim (POST /victim/register — victim.js)

This transaction is triggered when a logged-in non-victim user, for example a Volunteer or Dispatcher, registers themselves as a Victim to gain access to victim features. Inside the transaction, the route checks that the user exists and is not already in the Victim table, reuses the Emergency\_contact from the Volunteer table if the user is a volunteer, and inserts a new Victim row. After commit, a new JWT is issued with the updated roles array. A rollback occurs if the user is not found, if they are already a victim, or if an emergency contact is missing and cannot be reused from the Volunteer table.

19\. Public Emergency Request Flows (POST /victim/emergency, /victim/emergency/volunteer, /victim/emergency/admin, /victim/emergency/dispatcher — victim.js)

These four transactions share the same overall structure and are triggered by users who have forgotten their password but need to submit an emergency request without logging in. Identity is verified via user ID and email rather than a JWT. Inside the transaction, if the user is not yet in the Victim table they are auto-registered as one; the emergency contact is sourced from the request body for victims, admins, and dispatchers, and reused from the Volunteer table for volunteers. A round-robin dispatcher selection is then performed using FOR UPDATE locks on both the Dispatcher rows and the last Request\_log row to ensure consistent ordering across concurrent requests, and a new Request\_log record is inserted with a type of Emergency. A rollback occurs if the user identity check fails, if the role confirmation fails, if an emergency contact is required but not provided, if the emergency contact format is invalid, or if no available dispatchers exist. For the dispatcher variant specifically, the requesting dispatcher is excluded from the selection pool to prevent self-assignment.

20\. Victim Submits a Request While Logged In (POST /victim/request — victim.js)

This transaction is triggered when a logged-in victim submits a new emergency request from their dashboard. The structure mirrors the public emergency flow: a round-robin dispatcher is selected using FOR UPDATE on the Dispatcher rows and the last Request\_log row, and a new Request\_log record is inserted. A rollback occurs if no available dispatchers exist or on any database error.

21\. Admin Password Reset via Recovery Route (PUT /password-recovery/:id/reset-password — passwordRecovery.js)

This transaction is triggered when an admin resets a user password through the dedicated password recovery route rather than through the admin dashboard shortcut. It is functionally identical to the admin dashboard version covered in scenario 5, with the same FOR UPDATE select, the same assigned-admin ownership check, the same random password generation and hashing, and the same Processed status update. The rollback conditions are identical.

22\. Logout Transactions (POST /auth/logout — authRoutes.js)

Logout is transactional only for Admin, Volunteer, and Dispatcher roles; Victim logout is always permitted without a transaction.

For an Admin, the transaction acquires a FOR UPDATE lock on the Admin row, checks whether the admin has any Pending Password\_Recovery records assigned to them, and if clear sets Availability to No. A rollback occurs if pending recovery requests exist.

For a Volunteer, the transaction acquires a FOR UPDATE lock on the Volunteer row, checks for any non-Completed assignments via the Volunteer\_assignment and Assignment tables, and if clear sets Availability to No and Status to null. A rollback occurs if active assignments exist.

For a Dispatcher, the transaction acquires a FOR UPDATE lock on the Dispatcher row and checks three blocking conditions: Emergency-type Request\_log entries that have no associated Incident yet, Incident rows with a status other than Completed, and Assignment rows with a status other than Completed. If all three checks pass, Availability is set to No. A rollback occurs if any of the three blocking conditions returns rows.

**ACID properties**

**Atomicity**

Every multi-step write in the system is wrapped in an explicit START TRANSACTION and COMMIT pair, with a corresponding ROLLBACK in the catch block. For example, when an admin creates a user, both the User insert and the role-specific insert (such as Volunteer or Dispatcher) either commit together or roll back together, ensuring no half-created account can exist in the database. The same applies to password reset, where the User password update and the Password\_Recovery status update are bundled so neither can succeed without the other. The finally blocks throughout all route files ensure that connections are always released and locks are always freed regardless of whether the transaction succeeded or failed, preventing orphaned locks from blocking future operations.

**Consistency**

Consistency is enforced at multiple layers. At the schema level, CHECK constraints on tables such as Incident, Assignment, Volunteer, and Admin restrict column values to defined enumerations, for example Status can only be Completed, Ongoing, or Pending, and Availability can only be Yes or No. UNIQUE constraints on User.Email, User.CNIC, and User.Phone\_no prevent duplicate identity records. FOREIGN KEY constraints across the entire schema, such as Request\_log referencing both Victim and Dispatcher, and Volunteer\_assignment referencing both Volunteer and Assignment, enforce referential integrity so no orphaned child records can exist. At the application layer, validation before transactions open further reinforces consistency, for example checking that a referenced Centre exists before inserting a Volunteer or Dispatcher. Three triggers add a further layer. The check\_volunteer\_availability\_before\_insert trigger fires before every insert into Volunteer\_assignment and raises a SIGNAL if the volunteer's Availability is not Yes, meaning the database itself will reject an assignment even if the application-layer check were somehow bypassed. The set\_completion\_time\_on\_finish trigger automatically sets Completion\_time on the Assignment row when Status transitions to Completed, ensuring that field is never left null on a finished record. The update\_incident\_status\_on\_assignment trigger cascades that completion to the linked Incident row, keeping the two tables consistent without relying on the application to issue a second update.

**Isolation**

MySQL's default isolation level of REPEATABLE READ is used throughout. Where stronger guarantees are needed, SELECT ... FOR UPDATE is used explicitly to escalate to row-level exclusive locking within a transaction. This appears in the logout flows for Admin, Volunteer, and Dispatcher, where the role row is locked before checking blocking conditions, and in the password recovery assignment logic, where the last assigned Admin\_id row and the chosen admin's own row are both locked before the insert to prevent a race condition where two concurrent requests could select the same admin or where the chosen admin could log out between selection and insert. Advisory locks via GET\_LOCK and IS\_USED\_LOCK provide an additional isolation layer at the application level. The named write lock "admin\_write\_lock" serialises all admin write operations. Row-level advisory locks keyed as TableName\_RecordId prevent two dispatchers from editing the same victim or incident record concurrently. The admin priority system adds a further override where, if admin priority is active, dispatcher write operations are blocked entirely, and where an admin can force-release a lock held by a dispatcher using RELEASE\_LOCK before acquiring it themselves.

**Durability**

Durability is delegated to MySQL's InnoDB storage engine, which is used implicitly given that all tables are created without an explicit ENGINE clause and InnoDB is the default. InnoDB writes committed transactions to its redo log before acknowledging the commit, meaning that once a COMMIT returns successfully, the data survives a crash or restart. At the application level, the codebase never treats a write as durable until the COMMIT has returned without error. All response payloads, such as the success messages in user creation, password reset, and incident creation, are only sent after the commit, never speculatively after individual inserts.

Three indexes were created on the Incident and Assignment tables targeting the most common join and filter patterns in the system. A query against INFORMATION\_SCHEMA.STATISTICS beforehand confirmed that no non-primary indexes existed on any table, meaning every query was relying on primary key lookups or full table scans.

**Indexing & Performance**

**Index 1: idx\_assignment\_incident on Assignment(Incident\_id)**

Assignment.Incident\_id is joined to Incident in almost every dispatcher and admin query. MySQL does not automatically index foreign key columns on the child table, so without this index every query triggered a full scan of the Assignment table. After adding it, Query 1, which joins Assignment to Incident filtering by High severity, switched to a direct lookup using the index instead of reading every row. The EXPLAIN ANALYZE output before the index showed MySQL selecting Assignment as the full-scan driving table with a nested loop into Incident. After the index, MySQL used idx\_assignment\_incident as a ref lookup, eliminating the need to scan unrelated rows. Expected amount of improvement is 60 to 80 percent.

**Index 2: idx\_incident\_status on Incident(Status)**

Status is the most commonly filtered column in the system. Dispatcher workload checks, admin overview queries, logout checks, and delete safety checks all filter by Status, usually looking for Ongoing or Pending records. Without the index each of these caused a full table scan. Query 2 fetches all Pending and Ongoing incidents ordered by Time. Before the index, EXPLAIN ANALYZE showed a full table scan with a using where annotation, meaning every row was read and then discarded if it did not match. After the index, MySQL used idx\_incident\_status to go directly to matching rows, with a filesort still applied for the Time ordering but on a much smaller row set. Expected improvement is 70 to 90 percent, making this one the highest-impact index of the three.

**Index 3: idx\_incident\_request on Incident(Request\_id)**

Request\_id is a foreign key on Incident joined to Request\_log in both database views, all dispatcher incident fetches, and the dispatcher logout check. The same child-table indexing gap applies as in Index 1\. Query 3 joins the two tables filtering by Request\_time on or after a given date and Verification\_status equal to True. Before the index, EXPLAIN ANALYZE showed MySQL choosing one table as the full-scan driver and performing unindexed lookups into the other for each matching row. After adding idx\_incident\_request, MySQL could use the index to locate matching Incident rows directly from the Request\_log side, converting the unindexed lookup into a ref operation. Expected improvement is 50 to 75 percent, with the range depending on how selective the Request\_time date filter is.

**APIs**

**Auth (`/api/auth`)**

1. POST `/auth/register` — No auth — Register new victim account  
2. POST `/auth/login` — No auth — Login, returns JWT  
3. POST `/auth/logout` — JWT — Role-aware logout, blocks if active work pending

**Victim (`/api/victim`)**

1. POST `/victim/verify-identity` — No auth — Verify user\_id \+ email \+ role (emergency flow step 1\)  
2. POST `/victim/emergency` — No auth — Public emergency request for victims  
3. POST `/victim/emergency/volunteer` — No auth — Public emergency request for volunteers  
4. POST `/victim/emergency/admin` — No auth — Public emergency request for admins  
5. POST `/victim/emergency/dispatcher` — No auth — Public emergency request for dispatchers  
6. POST `/victim/register` — JWT — Add Victim role to existing account  
7. POST `/victim/request` — JWT (Victim) — Submit new emergency request  
8. GET `/victim/incidents` — JWT (Victim) — View own incidents

**Password Recovery (`/api/password-recovery`)**

1. POST `/password-recovery/forgot` — No auth — Public forgot-password, round-robins to available admin  
2. POST `/password-recovery` — JWT (Victim/Vol/Disp) — Logged-in recovery request  
3. GET `/password-recovery` — JWT (Admin) — View all recovery requests  
4. GET `/password-recovery/my-requests` — JWT (Victim/Vol/Disp) — View own requests  
5. PUT `/password-recovery/:id/reset-password` — JWT (Admin) — Reset user password, mark processed  
6. PUT `/password-recovery/:id` — JWT (Admin) — Update status or note  
7. DELETE `/password-recovery/:id` — JWT (Admin) — Delete request

**Dispatcher (`/api/dispatcher`)**

1. GET `/dispatcher/workload` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — Own stats and availability  
2. PUT `/dispatcher/availability` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — Toggle own availability  
3. GET `/dispatcher/victim/search` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — Search user by phone, user\_id, or email  
4. GET `/dispatcher/victim/:id` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — Get single victim detail  
5. PUT `/dispatcher/victim/:id` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — Update victim record  
6. POST `/dispatcher/call-log` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — Log a call, creates Request\_log row  
7. GET `/dispatcher/calls` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — List own calls  
8. GET `/dispatcher/calls/:id` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — Single call detail  
9. PUT `/dispatcher/calls/:id` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — Update call note or type  
10. POST `/dispatcher/incident` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — Create incident from request  
11. GET `/dispatcher/incidents` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — List own incidents  
12. GET `/dispatcher/incidents/:id` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — Single incident full detail  
13. PUT `/dispatcher/incident/:id` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — Update incident status, severity, or note  
14.  GET /dispatcher/volunteers/available — JWT (Disp/Admin) — Available volunteers across all centres  
15. POST `/dispatcher/incident/:id/assign-volunteers` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — Assign volunteers to incident  
16. GET `/dispatcher/incident/:id/volunteers` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — List volunteers on incident  
17. DELETE `/dispatcher/volunteer-assignment/:id` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — Remove volunteer, Pending assignments only  
18. GET `/dispatcher/resources/available` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — Available police and ambulance  
19. GET `/dispatcher/resources` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — All resources with statuses  
20. POST `/dispatcher/assignment` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — Create assignment with police, ambulance, volunteers  
21. PUT `/dispatcher/assignment/:id` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — Update assignment status  
22. GET `/dispatcher/incident/:id/backup-status` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — Backup history for incident  
23. POST `/dispatcher/volunteer/:id/backup-request` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — Process volunteer backup request  
24. GET `/dispatcher/backups` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — All active backup requests  
25. POST `/dispatcher/lawcase` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — Create law case  
26. GET `/dispatcher/lawcases` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — List own law cases  
27. PUT /dispatcher/lawcase/:id — JWT (Disp/Admin) — Update law case firm name or case type  
28. GET `/dispatcher/lawfirms` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — Previously used law firm names  
29. POST `/dispatcher/followup` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — Create follow-up support  
30. GET `/dispatcher/followups` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — List own follow-ups  
31. PUT /dispatcher/followup/:id — JWT (Disp/Admin) — Update follow-up referred centre or case type  
32. GET `/dispatcher/referred-centres` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — Previously used referred centres  
33.  GET /dispatcher/volunteers — JWT (Disp/Admin) — All volunteers across all centres  
34. GET `/dispatcher/centres` — JWT (Disp/Admin) — All centres  
35. POST `/dispatcher/incident/:id/admin-takeover` — JWT (Admin only) — Force-take incident lock  
36. POST `/dispatcher/incident/:id/admin-release-lock` — JWT (Admin only) — Release incident lock

**Admin (`/api/admin`)**

**Priority Lock**

1. POST `/admin/priority-lock` — JWT (Admin) — Activate dispatcher write block, 1 to 60 minutes  
2. POST `/admin/priority-unlock` — JWT (Admin) — Release write block  
3. GET `/admin/priority-lock-status` — JWT (Admin) — Check current lock state  
4. POST `/admin/system/claim-priority` — JWT (Admin) — Claim app-level priority lock  
5. POST `/admin/system/release-priority` — JWT (Admin) — Release own priority lock  
6. POST `/admin/system/force-release` — JWT (Admin) — Force-release any priority lock  
7. GET `/admin/system/lock-status` — JWT (Admin) — Full lock state for app and DB

**Overview**

1. GET `/admin/overview` — JWT (Admin) — System-wide counts and availability stats

**User Management**

1. POST `/admin/user` — JWT (Admin) — Create user of any role  
2. PUT `/admin/user/:id` — JWT (Admin) — Update user or change role  
3. DELETE `/admin/user/:id` — JWT (Admin) — Delete user with safety checks  
4. GET `/admin/users` — JWT (Admin) — List all users with roles  
5. GET `/admin/users/search` — JWT (Admin) — Search by name, email, role, phone, or DOB

**Dispatcher Oversight**

1. GET `/admin/dispatchers` — JWT (Admin) — All dispatchers with workload stats  
2. PUT `/admin/dispatcher/:id/availability` — JWT (Admin) — Set dispatcher availability  
3. PUT `/admin/incident/:id/reassign` — JWT (Admin) — Reassign incident to different dispatcher  
4. GET `/admin/incidents` — JWT (Admin) — All incidents across all dispatchers

**Volunteer Oversight**

1. GET `/admin/volunteers` — JWT (Admin) — All volunteers across all centres

**Victim Oversight**

1. GET `/admin/victims` — JWT (Admin) — All victims with active incident counts

**Password Recovery Management**

1. GET `/admin/recoveries` — JWT (Admin) — All recovery requests, filterable by status  
2. GET `/admin/recoveries/mine` — JWT (Admin) — Own assigned requests  
3. PUT `/admin/recovery/:id/reset-password` — JWT (Admin) — Generate and set new password  
4. DELETE `/admin/recovery/:id` — JWT (Admin) — Delete recovery request

**Table Browser**

1. GET `/admin/tables` — JWT (Admin) — List all DB tables with metadata  
2. GET `/admin/{table}` — JWT (Admin) — Browse all rows of any table  
3. GET `/admin/{table}/:id` — JWT (Admin) — Single record with lock info  
4. PUT `/admin/{table}/:id` — JWT (Admin) — Update record, editable tables only  
5. DELETE `/admin/{table}/:id` — JWT (Admin) — Delete record, editable tables only

**Known Issues & Limitations**

**Messaging system:** tho we had wanted to implement this too but due to time constraint we opted to ensure that the rest of the project works properly. In order to deal with that, the same victim can post another request  which will be assigned with the same dispatcher or if he is inactive then with another dispatcher.

**Creating victim at run time:** since our system follows a member only system as to ensure the user can view her history we couldn't implement creating a new user at run time through dispatcher since it would require the same amount of data as a normal automated registration and if not then we would need to create a new table just for this specific condition in order to ensure database integrity. We tried to implement this to the best of our abilities but ended up not using it in the end prioritizing consistency.

**Maps:** Real time location monitoring wasn't handled as it would require maps api increasing the complexity of the project.

**Notifications:** Shows notification in dispatcher for new request and volunteer arrived but only if dispatcher has already logged in notifications when dispatcher isnt logged in wont show.

**User identity:** although forget password is allowed but user must still remember their id and email otherwise would need to contact a centre (meaning irl calling not a part of system).
